title: International Hockey League (1945–2001)

infobox.value: (**IHL**) was a minor professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") league in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") that operated from 1945 to 2001. The IHL served as the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL)'s alternate [farm system](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") to the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") (AHL). After 56 years of operation, financial instability led to the league's demise. Six of the surviving seven teams merged into the AHL in 2001.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [International Hockey League (1945–2001)](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League_(1945%E2%80%932001)&redirect=no "International Hockey League (1945–2001)"))

1945–2001 North American ice hockey league

For other uses, see [International Hockey League (disambiguation)](/wiki/International_Hockey_League_(disambiguation) "International Hockey League (disambiguation)").

International Hockey League

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Sport | [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") |
| Founded | 1945 |
| Ceased | 2001 |
| Countries | United States Canada |
| Most titles | [Cincinnati Mohawks](/wiki/Cincinnati_Mohawks "Cincinnati Mohawks") (5) |

The **International Hockey League** (**IHL**) was a minor professional [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") league in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") that operated from 1945 to 2001. The IHL served as the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL)'s alternate [farm system](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") to the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") (AHL). After 56 years of operation, financial instability led to the league's demise. Six of the surviving seven teams merged into the AHL in 2001.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

The IHL was formed on December 5, 1945, in a three-hour meeting at the Norton Palmer Hotel in [Windsor, Ontario](/wiki/Windsor,_Ontario "Windsor, Ontario"). In attendance were [Jack Adams](/wiki/Jack_Adams "Jack Adams") (coach of the Detroit Red Wings), [Fred Huber](/wiki/Fred_Huber "Fred Huber") (Red Wings public relations), [Frank Gallagher](/wiki/Frank_Gallagher_(ice_hockey) "Frank Gallagher (ice hockey)") (amateur hockey organizer in Detroit and Windsor), [Lloyd Pollock](/wiki/Lloyd_Pollock "Lloyd Pollock") (Windsor hockey pioneer), Gerald McHugh (Windsor lawyer), Len Hebert, Len Loree and Bill Beckman. The league began operations in the [1945–46 IHL season](/wiki/1945%E2%80%9346_IHL_season "1945–46 IHL season") with four teams in Windsor and [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), and operated as semi-professional league.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-IHL-2)

In 1947, a team from [Toledo, Ohio](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio"), joined the league, and the following year the IHL expanded significantly, with teams in four additional U.S. cities. The expansion did not take hold, and for 1949–50, the league was back down to teams in Detroit and Windsor as well as two nearby Canadian cities, [Sarnia, Ontario](/wiki/Sarnia,_Ontario "Sarnia, Ontario"), and [Chatham, Ontario](/wiki/Chatham,_Ontario "Chatham, Ontario"). Windsor dropped out in 1950, and expansion into the U.S. began again, with Toledo rejoining the league and new teams in [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") (1950), [Troy, Ohio](/wiki/Troy_Bruins "Troy Bruins"), (1951), [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati") (1952), [Fort Wayne, Indiana](/wiki/Fort_Wayne,_Indiana "Fort Wayne, Indiana") (1952), and [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee") (1952). At the same time, the last Canadian team left the league in 1952, when the [Chatham Maroons](/wiki/Chatham_Maroons_(IHL) "Chatham Maroons (IHL)") pulled out. Three new U.S. cities were added in 1953. The league would expand and shrink between five and nine teams through the 1950s, with another major expansion in 1959. In the 1962–63 season, the IHL played an interlocking schedule with the NHL-owned [Eastern Professional Hockey League](/wiki/Eastern_Professional_Hockey_League_(1959-1963) "Eastern Professional Hockey League (1959-1963)"), which itself folded after its 1962–63 season. After 11 seasons as a strictly U.S.-based league, the IHL admitted two Canadian teams in 1963, with the [Windsor Bulldogs](/wiki/Windsor_Bulldogs "Windsor Bulldogs") and the return of the Chatham Maroons. Both teams dropped out after one season, however, and the league would not have a Canadian team again until 1996.

### Major market expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major market expansion")]

[Bill Beagan](/wiki/Bill_Beagan "Bill Beagan") served as commissioner of the IHL from 1969 to 1978. [The Canadian Press](/wiki/The_Canadian_Press "The Canadian Press") cited him for turning around the league's financial situation and making it a top-tier development system for future NHL talent.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Starting in the late 1960s, the IHL's quality of play significantly improved. By the mid-1970s it was on par with the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") (AHL), the longtime top feeder league for the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"). Many IHL teams became the top [farm teams](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") of NHL teams. In 1984, the league absorbed a few surviving members of the [Central Hockey League](/wiki/Central_Professional_Hockey_League "Central Professional Hockey League"), which had ceased operations.

In 1985, the league adopted the shootout to determine tie games in place of traditional overtime.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The NHL would begin using the shootout to avoid tie games in 2005.

Beginning in the late 1980s and continuing into the mid-90s, the IHL expanded or re-located existing franchises into major U.S. markets such as [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas,_Nevada "Las Vegas, Nevada"), [Minneapolis–Saint Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93Saint_Paul "Minneapolis–Saint Paul"), [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida"), [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona"), [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), [San Antonio](/wiki/San_Antonio,_Texas "San Antonio, Texas"), [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). Many of these markets had been previously served by the defunct [World Hockey Association](/wiki/World_Hockey_Association "World Hockey Association") or abandoned by the NHL. The IHL also entered markets that had existing NHL teams, such as Chicago, Detroit, and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). In 1996, the IHL moved its Atlanta and Minneapolis–Saint Paul franchises to [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") and [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), respectively, restoring the league's Canadian presence and filling the void left by the departure of the NHL's [Quebec Nordiques](/wiki/Quebec_Nordiques "Quebec Nordiques") and the original [Winnipeg Jets](/wiki/Winnipeg_Jets_(1972%E2%80%9396) "Winnipeg Jets (1972–96)").

The minimum requirements for an IHL expansion team in 1995 were "a 10,000-seat arena, a population base of one million, and a $6 million franchise fee."[[5]](#cite_note-5) As the league expanded into larger markets, many of the smaller-market teams (such as Fort Wayne, Peoria, Muskegon, Kalamazoo and Flint) left the IHL and joined lower-level leagues.

### Decline and collapse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Decline and collapse")]

The IHL's expansion into NHL markets put a strain on relationships between the leagues. There was some speculation that the IHL was intending to compete directly with the NHL, especially when a [lockout](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_NHL_lockout "1994–95 NHL lockout") in 1994–95 threatened to wipe out the NHL season.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, in the 1995–96 season, the IHL's "soft" [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") was just $1.5 million,[[7]](#cite_note-7) while the lowest NHL team payroll that season was $11.4 million.[[8]](#cite_note-8) A Fall 1994 article in *[Sports Illustrated](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated "Sports Illustrated")* praising the IHL and mocking the NHL only fueled the fire. In said article, IHL officials detailed plans to continue expanding the league to large markets in North America, as well as, "a six-team European league with franchises in England, Switzerland, Italy, Austria, Sweden and France."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In response, many NHL clubs shifted their affiliations to the [AHL](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League"), and by 1997–98, only four of 18 IHL teams had NHL affiliations.[[10]](#cite_note-10) With the loss of subsidized salaries, high expansion fees (by the end the league was charging as much as $8 million US for new teams), exploding travel costs and the NHL itself moving back into some of its markets, the league's rapid expansion proved a critical strain, and it folded after the 2000–01 season.

Six IHL franchises (the [Chicago Wolves](/wiki/Chicago_Wolves "Chicago Wolves"), [Grand Rapids Griffins](/wiki/Grand_Rapids_Griffins "Grand Rapids Griffins"), [Houston Aeros](/wiki/Houston_Aeros_(1994%E2%80%932013) "Houston Aeros (1994–2013)"), [Utah Grizzlies](/wiki/Utah_Grizzlies_(1995%E2%80%932005) "Utah Grizzlies (1995–2005)"), [Milwaukee Admirals](/wiki/Milwaukee_Admirals "Milwaukee Admirals") and [Manitoba Moose](/wiki/Manitoba_Moose "Manitoba Moose")) were admitted into the AHL as expansion teams for the 2001–02 season. Between them, they have played for the AHL [Calder Cup](/wiki/Calder_Cup "Calder Cup") seven times, winning four—including three in a row after their arrival. As well, the [Cincinnati Cyclones](/wiki/Cincinnati_Cyclones "Cincinnati Cyclones") was readmitted to the [East Coast Hockey League](/wiki/East_Coast_Hockey_League "East Coast Hockey League"), which hosted the team from 1990 to 1992 before it moved to the IHL. The [Orlando Solar Bears](/wiki/Orlando_Solar_Bears_(IHL) "Orlando Solar Bears (IHL)") (the final IHL playoff champions) and the [Kansas City Blades](/wiki/Kansas_City_Blades "Kansas City Blades") were not admitted into the AHL because their owner, [Rich DeVos](/wiki/Rich_DeVos "Rich DeVos"), who also owned the Griffins, was allowed to own only one AHL franchise. The league's other two teams, the [Cleveland Lumberjacks](/wiki/Cleveland_Lumberjacks "Cleveland Lumberjacks") and [Detroit Vipers](/wiki/Detroit_Vipers "Detroit Vipers"), ceased operations along with the league.

Two former IHL teams that moved to the AHL have since relocated: the Utah Grizzlies moved to [Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio"), to become the [Lake Erie Monsters](/wiki/Lake_Erie_Monsters "Lake Erie Monsters") (rebranded as Cleveland Monsters in 2016) in 2007 and the Houston Aeros moved to [Des Moines, Iowa](/wiki/Des_Moines,_Iowa "Des Moines, Iowa"), to become the [Iowa Wild](/wiki/Iowa_Wild "Iowa Wild") in 2013. A third team, the Manitoba Moose, temporarily relocated to [St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador") to become the [St. John's IceCaps](/wiki/St._John%27s_IceCaps "St. John's IceCaps") from 2011 to 2015. Three former franchises have been relaunched in lower-tier leagues since the IHL's demise. The [Utah Grizzlies](/wiki/Utah_Grizzlies "Utah Grizzlies") name was revived by the former [Lexington Men O' War](/wiki/Lexington_Men_O%27_War "Lexington Men O' War") of the ECHL when they moved, and the [Orlando Solar Bears](/wiki/Orlando_Solar_Bears_(ECHL) "Orlando Solar Bears (ECHL)") restarted as an ECHL expansion team. The [Peoria Rivermen](/wiki/Peoria_Rivermen_(IHL) "Peoria Rivermen (IHL)") have had three more franchises with an [expansion team](/wiki/Peoria_Rivermen_(ECHL) "Peoria Rivermen (ECHL)") in the [East Coast Hockey League](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") from 1996 to 2005, a relocated [Worcester IceCats](/wiki/Worcester_IceCats "Worcester IceCats") in the [American Hockey League](/wiki/American_Hockey_League "American Hockey League") from [2005 to 2013](/wiki/Peoria_Rivermen_(AHL) "Peoria Rivermen (AHL)"), and a fourth incarnation of the [Peoria Rivermen](/wiki/Peoria_Rivermen_(SPHL) "Peoria Rivermen (SPHL)") subsequently launched in the [Southern Professional Hockey League](/wiki/Southern_Professional_Hockey_League "Southern Professional Hockey League") in 2013.

## Trophies and awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Hockey_League&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Trophies and awards")]

[![Turner Cup trophy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Turner_Cup.jpg/250px-Turner_Cup.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turner_Cup.jpg)

The Turner Cup was the championship trophy of the International Hockey League.

| Award name | Seasons | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Turner Cup](/wiki/Turner_Cup "Turner Cup") | 1945–2001 | League playoff champions |
| [Fred A. Huber Trophy](/wiki/Fred_A._Huber_Trophy "Fred A. Huber Trophy") | 1945–2001 | Regular season champions |
| [Commissioner's Trophy](/wiki/Commissioner%27s_Trophy_(IHL) "Commissioner's Trophy (IHL)") | 1984–2001 | Coach of the year |
| [Leo P. Lamoureux Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Leo_P._Lamoureux_Memorial_Trophy "Leo P. Lamoureux Memorial Trophy") | 1946–2001 | Top point scorer. *Known as "George H. Wilkinson Trophy" (1946-1960).* |
| [James Gatschene Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Gatschene_Memorial_Trophy "James Gatschene Memorial Trophy") | 1946–2001 | MVP / Sportsmanship |
| [Norman R. "Bud" Poile Trophy](/wiki/Norman_R._%22Bud%22_Poile_Trophy_(IHL) "Norman R. \"Bud\" Poile Trophy (IHL)") | 1988–2001 | Playoffs MVP |
| [Gary F. Longman Memorial Trophy](/wiki/Gary_F._Longman_Memorial_Trophy "Gary F. Longman Memorial Trophy") | 1961–2001 | Rookie of the year *Known as "Leading Rookie Award" (1961–1967).* |
| [Ken McKenzie Trophy](/wiki/Ken_McKenzie_Trophy_(IHL) "Ken McKenzie Trophy (IHL)") | 1977–2001 | American-born rookie of the year |
| [Governor's Trophy](/wiki/Governor%27s_Trophy "Governor's Trophy") | 1964–2001 | Best defenseman. *Known as "Larry D. Gordon Trophy" (1998–2001).* |
| [James Norris Memorial Trophy](/wiki/James_Norris_Memorial_Trophy_(IHL) "James Norris Memorial Trophy (IHL)") | 1955–2001 | Goaltenders with lowest [GAA](/wiki/Goals_against_average "Goals against average"). |
| [John Cullen Award](/wiki/John_Cullen_Award "John Cullen Award") | 1996–2001 | Comeback player of the year. *Known as "Comeback Player of the Year Award" (1996–1998).* |
| [Ironman Award](/wiki/Ironman_Award "Ironman Award") | 1988–2001 | Durability / longevity. |
| [IHL Man of the Year](/wiki/IHL_Man_of_the_Year "IHL Man of the Year") | 1992–2001 | Outstanding community service. *Also known as "I. John Snider, II Trophy."* |

* [Source](https://www.hockeydb.com/ihdb/awards/displayawardleague.php?league_id=IHL1946).
